title: Q3SAT-GPT: A Generative Model for Discovering Quantum Circuits for the 3-SAT Problem
authors: Pratim Ugale, Ilya Tyagin, Karunya Shirali, Kien X. Nguyen, Ilya Safro
abstract: This work introduces Q3SAT-GPT, a generative model for discovering quantum circuits for the Max-E3-SAT problem. Our method learns from high-performing QAOA-style ansätze to directly generate candidate circuits. To create high-quality supervision, we also introduce Mosaic Adaptive QAOA (MosaicADAPT-QAOA), an adaptive strategy for constructing low-depth QAOA circuits by selecting subsets of mixer operators in each step, rather than inserting operators sequentially. The resulting circuits serve as training data for the generative model, allowing it to learn effective circuit design patterns while eliminating the need for costly variational optimization at inference time. Experiments show that our framework attains strong solution quality with shallow circuits and scales significantly better than both our adaptive construction procedure and conventional variational baselines. Our results establish generative modeling as a high-performance route toward the scalable discovery of quantum optimization circuits, demonstrating that these models can effectively internalize circuit logic while providing a foundation for future, instance-aware inductive biases. Reproducibility: The source code is available at https://github.com/pratimugale/Q3SAT-GPT.
summary: This paper introduces Q3SAT-GPT, a generative AI model that learns to design quantum circuits for solving 3-SAT problems. By training on circuits generated by a novel adaptive method (MosaicADAPT-QAOA), the model can bypass expensive classical optimization during inference, providing a scalable route for quantum algorithm discovery.
main_problem: The high computational cost and complexity of discovering optimized quantum circuits for the Max-E3-SAT combinatorial optimization problem using traditional adaptive QAOA methods.
main_result: The Q3SAT-GPT generative model can predict high-quality quantum circuits in a single forward pass, achieving significant speedups in amortized inference time while maintaining strong solution quality.
method: A transformer-based generative architecture (Q3SAT-GPT) is trained on high-quality circuit trajectories produced by a new adaptive strategy called MosaicADAPT-QAOA, which uses a Maximum Weight Independent Set approach to select disjoint operators.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the Max-E3-SAT problem, mapping classical 3-CNF formulas to a cost Hamiltonian using a Higher-Order Unconstrained Binary Optimization (HUBO) formulation with Pauli-Z operators.
key_observables: Approximation Ratio (AR), Error Rate (ER) of circuit validity, and amortized inference time.
important_parameters: Phase transition ratio (alpha = m/n), circuit depth, and initial variational angles (gamma_0, beta_0).
main_assumptions: The model's performance is limited by the quality of the training data from MosaicADAPT-QAOA, and it assumes the 3-SAT instances can be effectively represented via Literal-Clause Graph (LCG) embeddings.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the failure of greedy operator selection; Figure 2 depicts an incompatibility graph; Figure 3 illustrates the transformer pipeline and embeddings; Figure 4 shows energy convergence; Figure 7 compares predicted vs. optimized variational parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of circuit discovery, presents the MosaicADAPT-QAOA adaptive strategy, details the Q3SAT-GPT generative architecture and training pipeline, evaluates performance on random and balanced 3-SAT instances, and discusses scalability and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: A Representative Conflict Illustration. Operator gener- ators ˆAi are labeled with their corresponding gradient mag- nitudes in parentheses. In (a), selecting the highest individual gradient ( ˆA1) blocks the selection possibility of ˆA4 and ˆA5, leading to a lower total sum of gradients as compared to (b).

Fig. 2: Incompatibility graph representation for a 3-qubit problem instance, used for operator selection. Each node in the graph represents a candidate unitary operator from the pool P, An edge connects two nodes if and only if their corresponding operators have overlapping qubit support. Sets of overlapping two-qubit operators form fully connected cliques and are omitted to maintain legibility.
Fig. 3: Detailed pipeline demonstrating formatting, tokenization, and embeddings. The | delimiter is used to segment clauses, with explicit OR (∨) tokens omitted.

Fig. 4: Comparison of the energy convergence across adap- tations. Note that in Figure (b), plots of TETRIS-QAOA and ADAPT-QAOA overlap due to the same operators being chosen by both the methods (see Figure 6).

Fig. 5: Comparison of the maximum gradient norm across all operators in the mixer pool. Note that in Figure (b), plots of TETRIS-QAOA and ADAPT-QAOA overlap due to the same operators having the maximum gradient for the most part (see Figure 6).
